label: delphi-r2-p1
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 70,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not a court finds both halves of the compound proposition true: (1) the councilmembers' Signal messages about franchise terms, diversion targets, \"what council will accept,\" and RFP timing are \"public records\" under the content-controls-not-location rule of City of San Jose v. Superior Court, and (2) the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer \u2014 issued after searching only city systems, with no inquiry to the three individual councilmembers about personal-device/account communications, despite a request expressly covering \"any platform or account\" \u2014 falls short of the reasonably-calculated-search standard that San Jose and its Court of Appeal remand decision require, and thus violated the Act. The compound (conjunctive) nature of the proposition and some residual doctrinal wiggle room on \"used/retained\" and on how courts characterize inadequate-search liability keep this from being a near-certainty.",
  "rationales": [
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 7920.530 (former \u00a7 6252(e)) defines 'public records' by content and function ('relating to the conduct of the public's business,' 'prepared, owned, used, or retained') without reference to the account or device on which a writing sits, and the messages described \u2014 franchise terms, diversion targets, political vote-counting, RFP timing \u2014 sit squarely within that functional definition.",
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 directly rejected a categorical exemption for city officials' communications on personal accounts/devices and held that content, not location, is the touchstone, making this fact pattern (officials discussing franchise business on personal phones) close to the paradigm case the opinion was written to address.",
    "The CPRA's duty-to-assist and reasonable-search obligations (Gov. Code \u00a7 7922.600, former \u00a7 6253.1; see also Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 on search adequacy) require an inquiry reasonably calculated to locate records, and where the request expressly demanded communications 'on any platform or account,' confining the search to city servers is a strong candidate for a per-se inadequate search under the Court of Appeal's remand decision in San Jose (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, which contemplated that agencies must at least ask custodians to search personal accounts.",
    "Existence of ~40 surviving screenshots supplies concrete evidentiary proof of content and participation that removes speculative-record problems a court might otherwise use to avoid reaching the public-record question, making a definitive (rather than remanded/deferred) holding on prong one more likely here than in a case with no surviving evidence.",
    "The proposition is conjunctive and thus more fragile than either prong alone; a court could plausibly find the messages qualify as public records yet still excuse the initial search as facially reasonable given ambiguity (as of the response date) about whether the CPRA reaches messages on a third-party-hosted app like Signal, which would defeat the proposition even though prong one succeeds."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 7920.530 (former \u00a7 6252(e)) \u2014 definition of 'public records'",
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608",
    "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision on search-adequacy protocol)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 7922.600 (former \u00a7 6253.1) \u2014 agency duty to assist requester / reasonably describe records",
    "Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 \u2014 reasonable-search standard",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 34090 \u2014 retention schedule (relevant to surrounding facts, not the core public-record definition)"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court treats these consultant-initiated, ephemeral Signal messages as sufficiently 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' by the councilmembers in their official capacity to satisfy San Jose's test \u2014 as opposed to characterizing them as informal, quasi-political/lobbying chatter outside the CPRA's reach \u2014 since San Jose itself involved the official's own texts/emails rather than a third-party-hosted group chat, and no published California decision squarely addresses a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 78.0, mean 76.9, std dev 3.2, range 70.0–80.0, middle 50% of panel between 76.0 and 80.0.

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — Content-controls-not-location (San Jose prong one).** *7 of 7 panelists.* Every panelist grounded the "public record" determination in *City of San Jose*'s content-based test, and every panelist characterized the subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," RFP timing) as core conduct of the public's business.

**Theme B — Duty to inquire of individual custodians / reasonable-search standard.** *7 of 7 panelists.* All invoked some version of a reasonably-calculated or reasonable-diligence search standard and noted the clerk's failure to ask any of the three councilmembers about personal-account communications. Panelists differed on the doctrinal source (San Jose remand instructions, § 6253.1/§ 7922.600 duty-to-assist, AB 700, case law on search adequacy) — see Theme E and Disagreement point 2.

**Theme C — "Any platform or account" request language as notice.** *5 of 7 panelists* (probabilities 70, 76, 78, 78, 80). These panelists argued the request's express wording put the clerk on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, with some describing the resulting search as "facially inadequate" or a candidate for a per-se violation.

**Theme D — The ~40 surviving screenshots.** *7 of 7 panelists, but used in two distinct ways:*
- *6 panelists* (76 through 80) treated the screenshots as making "no responsive records" factually inaccurate at the moment it was given — an independent basis for a violation regardless of the deleted messages.
- *1 panelist* (70) used the screenshots differently: as evidentiary proof of content and participation that allows a court to reach a definitive holding on the public-record question, rather than as proof of response falsity.

**Theme E — AB 700 statutory codification of personal-account search duties.** *2 of 7 panelists* (76, 78) cited AB 700 as codifying an affirmative duty to route requests to individual officials — though with different section numbers and chapter citations (see Disagreement point 5).

**Theme F — Conjunctive fragility of the proposition.** *5 of 7 panelists* (70, 76, 78, 78, 80) expressly noted that the proposition requires both prongs to hold, making it more fragile than either prong alone; the second (search-adequacy) prong was uniformly identified as the weaker link among these panelists.

**Theme G — Clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense.** *3 of 7 panelists* (76, 76, 78) — a mid-range cluster — flagged that a court could excuse the initial search as reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the Signal group at the time of response.

**Theme H — Majority-bloc participation strengthens the public-business nexus.** *3 of 7 panelists* (78, 80, 80) — skewing toward higher-probability panelists — emphasized that three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) participating in substantive deliberation reinforces both the content nexus and the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element.

**Theme I — Deleted-messages scope problem.** *2 of 7 panelists* (both at 80 — the highest-probability panelists) raised the possibility that a court might limit its "public records" holding to the surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of auto-deleted messages as a retention/spoliation issue rather than a CPRA determination.

**Theme J — Availability of declaratory relief despite destruction.** *1 panelist* (76) noted that CPRA declaratory/injunctive relief does not require that responsive records presently exist.

**Theme K — Possible exemptions even if records qualify.** *1 panelist* (76) noted content could be found preliminary/deliberative and exempt from disclosure despite public-record status.

**Theme L — Third-party/consultant-hosted platform distinction.** *1 panelist* (70 — the lowest-probability panelist) flagged that San Jose involved the official's own texts/emails, whereas this involves a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform, with no published California decision on point.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials.** Some panelists treat the request's "any platform or account" language as itself sufficient to trigger the duty (making the city-systems-only search per se or facially inadequate); others posit that absent actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group, the search could be found reasonable "at the time." Several panelists hold both positions in tension within their own answers, but as between panelists the trigger assumption is incompatible: request-language-alone versus knowledge-required.

2. **The state of post-San Jose search-methodology doctrine.** The 70 panelist cites the 2018 Court of Appeal remand decision (*City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013) as published authority contemplating that agencies must ask custodians to search personal accounts. Other panelists (78, 78, 80) describe search methodology as expressly left unresolved by San Jose, with no binding appellate authority fixing the duty's contours. These are incompatible readings of the same doctrinal landscape.

3. **Scope of any "public records" holding as to the deleted messages.** The two 80 panelists assume a court might confine its holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and recharacterize the rest as a retention/spoliation matter; other panelists' analyses assume the full body of Signal messages is subject to the public-record determination.

4. **Whether the Signal platform's characteristics matter.** The 70 panelist treats the consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, ephemeral nature of the channel as a genuine open question under the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element; other panelists (78, 80) describe these facts as a *cleaner* fit for San Jose than San Jose's own facts, treating the platform's characteristics as immaterial.

5. **What Gov. Code § 7922.545 is.** One panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the AB 700 personal-account search provision (Stats. 2021, ch. 132); another panelist (76) cites § 7922.545 as the recodified preliminary-drafts exemption (former § 6254(a)); a third panelist (78) cites the AB 700 provision as § 6252.5 (Stats. 2021, ch. 456). These citations are mutually inconsistent as to section number and chapter, and multiple panelists flagged their own citations for verification.

6. **Per se violation versus fact-bound adequacy question.** Panelists split on whether the "no responsive records" response would be treated as a clean statutory violation or as a fact-intensive search-adequacy question on which the city could partially prevail through a good-faith showing.

## 4. Authorities Cited

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 34090 (local agency records retention/destruction) — **7 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6252(e) / § 7920.530 (definition of "public records") — **4 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253.1 / § 7922.600 (duty to assist requester) — **3 panelists**
- *ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court* (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 (search adequacy) — **3 panelists**
- Gov. Code § 6253 / § 7922.500 / § 7922.530 (duty to make records available) — **3 panelists**
- AB 700 personal-account search provisions (cited variously as § 7922.545 and § 6252.5) — **2 panelists** (with inconsistent section/chapter citations; see Disagreement point 5)
- CPRA judicial-enforcement provisions (§ 7923.100, cited variously as former § 6259 and former § 6258) — **2 panelists**

**Cited by one panelist each:**
- *City of San Jose v. Superior Court* (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision)
- *Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385
- *Anderson-Barker v. Superior Court* (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 528 (flagged by the citing panelist as an analog to verify)
- Gov. Code § 7920.545 / former § 6252(g) (definition of "writing")
- Gov. Code § 7922.545 / former § 6254(a) (cited as preliminary drafts/notes exemption)
- Gov. Code § 7923.115 / former § 6259 (fee-shifting)
- Gov. Code § 7920.000 et seq. (general CPRA recodification citation)

Several panelists flagged their own recodified section numbers for verification; those flags are preserved above without correction.

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":77,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Revising upward from 70 to 77. The panel's Theme D point is the one I underweighted in Round 1 and now find decisive: the ~40 screenshots were preserved by the uneasy councilmember before auto-deletion, and nothing in the facts suggests they were later destroyed, so they almost certainly still existed on a personal device at the time Pacific Refuse's CPRA request was made and answered. That means the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer was not merely the product of an arguably-reasonable-but-narrow search — it was factually false as to a known, existing, non-hypothetical set of responsive franchise-related writings, independent of the harder question of what happens to the auto-deleted messages. That is a cleaner, more certain violation theory than the search-methodology debate I leaned on in Round 1. Combined with the strong, largely undisputed case that San Jose's content-based test reaches franchise-related group-chat content regardless of who hosts the platform, both prongs of the conjunctive proposition clear \"more likely than not\" by a wider margin than I credited before.","rationales":["Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) and City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 establish that content and function, not the device/account/platform on which a writing sits, determine public-record status; franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will accept,' and RFP timing are core conduct-of-the-public's-business content that a councilmember 'uses' by receiving and participating in the discussion, regardless of who created the Signal channel — this undercuts my Round 1 hesitation (Theme L) that the consultant-hosted, third-party-platform facts meaningfully distinguish this case from San Jose's own facts.","The surviving ~40 screenshots supply an independent, low-uncertainty basis for finding the 'no responsive records' answer inaccurate: unlike disappearing messages, screenshots are static and nothing in the facts indicates they were destroyed before the CPRA response, so at the moment the clerk answered, responsive records almost certainly existed on a personal device the clerk never searched — this is a stronger and more certain violation theory than relying solely on unsettled search-adequacy doctrine for the deleted messages.","The request's express language ('on any platform or account') gave the clerk at least constructive notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, and confining the search to city servers in the face of that language is a strong candidate for inadequate search under the duty-to-assist/reasonable-search line of authority (Gov. Code § 7922.600; Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385; and, more contestably, the San Jose remand decision, 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, on which the panel's Disagreement Point 2 shows genuine doctrinal uncertainty).","Three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) substantively participating in deliberation over franchise terms strengthens both the public-business nexus for prong one and the argument that the clerk should have anticipated personal-device records existed, cutting against a 'no actual/constructive knowledge' defense (Theme G) — even a generic duty-to-assist inquiry to councilmembers, which the clerk skipped entirely, would likely have surfaced the group's existence.","The proposition remains conjunctive and therefore more fragile than either prong alone, and genuine unresolved doctrine remains on (a) whether a court treats the search failure as a per se violation versus a fact-bound adequacy question allowing a good-faith defense, and (b) whether a court confines any 'public records' holding to the surviving screenshots and treats the bulk of destroyed messages as a retention/spoliation matter outside the CPRA determination itself (Theme I) — both cut somewhat against full confidence even after the upward revision."],"authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) — definition of 'public records'","Gov. Code § 7922.600 (former § 6253.1) — agency duty to assist requester","Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 — reasonable-search standard","City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 — Court of Appeal remand decision (search-methodology contours contested; verify)","Gov. Code § 34090 — retention schedule (relevant to surrounding facts, not core public-record definition)","AB 700 personal-account search provisions, Stats. 2021 — cited by panelists with inconsistent section numbers (§ 7922.545 vs. § 6252.5); verify correct citation"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court would treat the surviving screenshots as sufficient, on their own, to render the 'no responsive records' response a clear violation (a fairly determinate factual/legal question), versus whether it would instead fold the whole analysis into an unsettled, fact-bound search-adequacy inquiry that credits the clerk's lack of actual knowledge of the Signal group and treats the response as a good-faith (if ultimately incomplete) effort — the panel's Disagreement Points 1 and 2 show this trigger-for-duty-to-inquire question is genuinely unresolved in post-San Jose case law, and it is the main variable standing between my estimate and a higher (80+) probability.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"Theme D moved me most: six of seven panelists treated the surviving ~40 screenshots as an independent, near-certain basis for finding the 'no responsive records' answer false/violative, distinct from the harder question of what to do about the deleted messages. In Round 1 I used the screenshots only as evidence supporting the public-records characterization (prong one) and didn't give them full weight as a standalone violation theory for prong two; that was an underweighting on my part, and correcting it pushes my estimate up several points. Theme H (majority-bloc participation) and the panel's general treatment of the platform/ephemeral-app distinction as immaterial (contra my Round 1 Theme L concern) also moved me — on reflection the 'used' prong of San Jose doesn't turn on who owns the chat software. I did not fully adopt the panel median (78) because Disagreement Points 1–2 and 6 persuade me the search-methodology doctrine genuinely is unsettled post-San Jose (I'm not convinced the 2018 remand decision fixes a clear personal-account-inquiry rule), and because the conjunctive structure of the proposition still carries real fragility — so I land at 77, just under the median rather than at or above it."}

data.position_summary: Revising upward from 70 to 77. The panel's Theme D point is the one I underweighted in Round 1 and now find decisive: the ~40 screenshots were preserved by the uneasy councilmember before auto-deletion, and nothing in the facts suggests they were later destroyed, so they almost certainly still existed on a personal device at the time Pacific Refuse's CPRA request was made and answered. That means the clerk's "no responsive records" answer was not merely the product of an arguably-reasonable-but-narrow search — it was factually false as to a known, existing, non-hypothetical set of responsive franchise-related writings, independent of the harder question of what happens to the auto-deleted messages. That is a cleaner, more certain violation theory than the search-methodology debate I leaned on in Round 1. Combined with the strong, largely undisputed case that San Jose's content-based test reaches franchise-related group-chat content regardless of who hosts the platform, both prongs of the conjunctive proposition clear "more likely than not" by a wider margin than I credited before.
rationales: Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) and City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 establish that content and function, not the device/account/platform on which a writing sits, determine public-record status; franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will accept,' and RFP timing are core conduct-of-the-public's-business content that a councilmember 'uses' by receiving and participating in the discussion, regardless of who created the Signal channel — this undercuts my Round 1 hesitation (Theme L) that the consultant-hosted, third-party-platform facts meaningfully distinguish this case from San Jose's own facts.

The surviving ~40 screenshots supply an independent, low-uncertainty basis for finding the 'no responsive records' answer inaccurate: unlike disappearing messages, screenshots are static and nothing in the facts indicates they were destroyed before the CPRA response, so at the moment the clerk answered, responsive records almost certainly existed on a personal device the clerk never searched — this is a stronger and more certain violation theory than relying solely on unsettled search-adequacy doctrine for the deleted messages.

The request's express language ('on any platform or account') gave the clerk at least constructive notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, and confining the search to city servers in the face of that language is a strong candidate for inadequate search under the duty-to-assist/reasonable-search line of authority (Gov. Code § 7922.600; Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385; and, more contestably, the San Jose remand decision, 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, on which the panel's Disagreement Point 2 shows genuine doctrinal uncertainty).

Three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) substantively participating in deliberation over franchise terms strengthens both the public-business nexus for prong one and the argument that the clerk should have anticipated personal-device records existed, cutting against a 'no actual/constructive knowledge' defense (Theme G) — even a generic duty-to-assist inquiry to councilmembers, which the clerk skipped entirely, would likely have surfaced the group's existence.

The proposition remains conjunctive and therefore more fragile than either prong alone, and genuine unresolved doctrine remains on (a) whether a court treats the search failure as a per se violation versus a fact-bound adequacy question allowing a good-faith defense, and (b) whether a court confines any 'public records' holding to the surviving screenshots and treats the bulk of destroyed messages as a retention/spoliation matter outside the CPRA determination itself (Theme I) — both cut somewhat against full confidence even after the upward revision.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
Gov. Code § 7922.600 (former § 6253.1) — agency duty to assist requester
Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 — reasonable-search standard
City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 — Court of Appeal remand decision (search-methodology contours contested; verify)
Gov. Code § 34090 — retention schedule (relevant to surrounding facts, not core public-record definition)
AB 700 personal-account search provisions, Stats. 2021 — cited by panelists with inconsistent section numbers (§ 7922.545 vs. § 6252.5); verify correct citation
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court would treat the surviving screenshots as sufficient, on their own, to render the 'no responsive records' response a clear violation (a fairly determinate factual/legal question), versus whether it would instead fold the whole analysis into an unsettled, fact-bound search-adequacy inquiry that credits the clerk's lack of actual knowledge of the Signal group and treats the response as a good-faith (if ultimately incomplete) effort — the panel's Disagreement Points 1 and 2 show this trigger-for-duty-to-inquire question is genuinely unresolved in post-San Jose case law, and it is the main variable standing between my estimate and a higher (80+) probability.
data.response_to_panel: Theme D moved me most: six of seven panelists treated the surviving ~40 screenshots as an independent, near-certain basis for finding the 'no responsive records' answer false/violative, distinct from the harder question of what to do about the deleted messages. In Round 1 I used the screenshots only as evidence supporting the public-records characterization (prong one) and didn't give them full weight as a standalone violation theory for prong two; that was an underweighting on my part, and correcting it pushes my estimate up several points. Theme H (majority-bloc participation) and the panel's general treatment of the platform/ephemeral-app distinction as immaterial (contra my Round 1 Theme L concern) also moved me — on reflection the 'used' prong of San Jose doesn't turn on who owns the chat software. I did not fully adopt the panel median (78) because Disagreement Points 1–2 and 6 persuade me the search-methodology doctrine genuinely is unsettled post-San Jose (I'm not convinced the 2018 remand decision fixes a clear personal-account-inquiry rule), and because the conjunctive structure of the proposition still carries real fragility — so I land at 77, just under the median rather than at or above it.
